coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived carbon quantum dots as a versatile platform for fluorescent sensing, catalytic reduction, fluorescent ink and anticancer agents
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K.
Mathew, Jincy
K, Sreekanth
K, Radhakrishnan E.
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: A simple and green hydrothermal approach was used to fabricate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the rhizomes of Acorus calamus with a quantum yield (QY) of 15 %. Designed CQDs were applied for four different applications, including the fluorescence detection of morin, the catalytic reduction of rhodamine B (Rh B) and sunset yellow (SY), fluorescent ink for writing and drawing, and anticancer and apoptosis studies on SKMEL-28 human skin cancer cell lines. Several characterization methods were performed to explore the optical, structural, and morphological properties. The average particle size of the CQDs was 6 nm, with a blue fluorescence emission when exposed to UV light under 360 nm. Based on the static and inner filter effects (IFE), the developed radiometric nanosensor showed high selectivity towards morin with a linear range of 0–2 μM and a detection limit of 96 nM. Using a NaBH4-CQD mixture, the catalytic reduction of the ionic dyes Rh B and SY was achieved within seconds. A pseudo-first-order kinetics was followed by the reaction, with a rate constant of 0.0116 Min−1. The cytotoxic studies revealed the anticancer ability of CQDs on SKMEL 28 cell lines with a low LD50 value of 102 μg/mL. Besides the apoptosis studies using the double staining method show both viable and non-viable cells present in the medium. This study emphasizes the multiple practical uses of CQDs and demonstrates their versatility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Morin
Catalytic reduction
Anticancer study
Apoptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of fluorescent detection of morin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the Senior Research Fellowship provided by UGC India (Grant Number BININ01764209 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical behaviour of CQDs; (a) UV–vis. absorption spectra CQDs with fluorescence excitation and emission. Inset shows CQD solution under daylight (brown) and UV light (blue), (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at various excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) TEM image, (b) HR-TEM, (c) SAED, and (d) particle size distribution diagram of CQDs.
(a) FT-IR, (b) XRD, and (c) Raman spectrum of CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs, (b) high-resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C1s, and (c) O1s.

(a) Fluorescence behaviour of CQDs after mixing with various analytes, (b) zeta potential analysis of CQDs, (c) zeta potential analysis of CQD-PA mixture, (d) fluorescence behaviour of CQDs on increasing the morin concentration, and (e) F0/F vs concentration of morin plot.
(a) Selectivity analysis of morin in the presence of other interfering analytes, and (b) fluorescence lifetime analysis.

Catalytic reduction of rhodamine B in (a) NaBH4 alone, (b) NaBH4-CQD mixture, catalytic reduction of sunset yellow in (c) NaBH4 alone, and (d) NaBH4-CQD mixture, and (e) kinetic study. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescent images drawn using the developed CQDs as ink.
Fluorescence microscopic images of SKMEL 28 cells incubated for 24 h with various concentrations of CQDs (a) 0, (b) 6.25, (c) 12.5, (d) 25, (e) 50, and (f) 100 μg/mL using MTT assay.

Apoptosis images SKMEL 28 cells using the DNA-binding dyes acridine orange and ethidium bromide in the (a) absence, and (b) presence of CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the various processes involved in the synthesis of CQDs.
Mechanism of catalytic reduction of ionic dyes using NaBH4-CQD mixture.
A comparison of the present work with some of the recently published works.
Fluorescent detection of morin in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived carbon quantum dots as a versatile platform for fluorescent sensing, catalytic reduction, fluorescent ink and anticancer agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam-686 560, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kuriakose Elias College, Mannanam, Kottayam, 686 561, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biosciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biosciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam-686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and green hydrothermal approach was used to fabricate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from the rhizomes of Acorus calamus with a quantum yield (QY) of 15 %. Designed CQDs were applied for four different applications, including the fluorescence detection of morin, the catalytic reduction of rhodamine B (Rh B) and sunset yellow (SY), fluorescent ink for writing and drawing, and anticancer and apoptosis studies on SKMEL-28 human skin cancer cell lines. Several characterization methods were performed to explore the optical, structural, and morphological properties. The average particle size of the CQDs was 6 nm, with a blue fluorescence emission when exposed to UV light under 360 nm. Based on the static and inner filter effects (IFE), the developed radiometric nanosensor showed high selectivity towards morin with a linear range of 0–2 μM and a detection limit of 96 nM. Using a NaBH4-CQD mixture, the catalytic reduction of the ionic dyes Rh B and SY was achieved within seconds. A pseudo-first-order kinetics was followed by the reaction, with a rate constant of 0.0116 Min−1. The cytotoxic studies revealed the anticancer ability of CQDs on SKMEL 28 cell lines with a low LD50 value of 102 μg/mL. Besides the apoptosis studies using the double staining method show both viable and non-viable cells present in the medium. This study emphasizes the multiple practical uses of CQDs and demonstrates their versatility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles exhibit unique chemical, electronic, and spectroscopic behaviour and are widely utilized in a variety of fields. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles that exhibit remarkable water solubility, biocompatibility, fluorescent behaviour, and low harmfulness [1]. Compared to the traditional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) like Zn, P, Si, Sn, Mn, Cd, Te, etc., CQDs show unique characteristics such as easy and low-cost synthesis, tunable optical properties, zero-dimensional size, chemical inertness, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [2–5]. CQDs are generally synthesized using either top-down or bottom-up approaches. The top-down method consists of arc discharge, chemical oxidation, and laser ablation techniques, and the bottom-up process includes pyrolysis, hydrothermal, and microwave-assisted techniques [6–10]. Among them, the hydrothermal method is one of the most widely used because of its ease, fast surface tuning, and excellent control over the morphology of the developed quantum dots. CQDs derived from natural carbon sources are considered green since the precursor materials are inexpensive and eco-friendly.

The Acorus calamus is a flowering plant belonging to the Acoraceae family. This plant is widely found in India, central Asia, North America and European countries and possesses several medicinal properties. The rhizomes of Acorus calamus were used as the carbon source in this study, which contains phytochemicals beta-asarone, methyl isoeugenol, alpha-asarone, saponins, lectins, sesquiterpenoids, lignans, and steroids [11,12]. The important pharmacological activities of Acorus calamus rhizome include medicines for gastrointestinal problems, sedatives, relieving pain, antioxidant, antimicrobial, anticancer and antidiabetic properties [13–15].

Morin is a kind of polyphenolic natural flavonoid with chemical formula 1,3,5,7,2′,4′-pentahydroxyflavanone, widely found in vegetables, fruits and herbal plants [16]. The medicinal use of morin includes anti-inflammatory, anti-tumour, anticancer and antioxidant activities. Hence, monitoring the morin level in the human body is essential and significant. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical methods and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques are available for the detection of morin [17–19]. However, intricate pretreatment, complex procedures, and the involvement of sophisticated instruments led to the need for a simple method for detecting morin. For this purpose, fluorescence-based detection methods are most attractive due to their low cost, fast response, easy operation, excellent selectivity, and good sensitivity.

The wide use of organic dyes in textiles, plastics, paints and parasitical industries, and their direct disposal to soil and water sources without proper treatment, is a severe threat to the environment because of their toxic nature towards terrestrial and aquatic animals. Hence, treating sewage water containing organic dyes is essential before discharging it into the surroundings. Several methods, such as Fenton oxidation, photocatalytic degradation and membrane separation, are currently being used for their treatments. These methods have some limitations, including high cost, use of sophisticated instruments, time consumption etc. [20,21].

The CQDs are very effective as fluorescent ink due to their excellent fluorescence, high quantum yield (QY), and easy availability. They are widely studied for fluorescent writing, drawing, anti-counterfeiting and anti-spurious applications. Recently, the unique optical properties and intrinsic therapeutic behaviour of CQDs have been deeply explored for applications in the health field, such as bioimaging, antimicrobial and anticancer activities [22].

In this work, the tunable fluorescent emission behaviour of CQDs was utilized for the label-free sensitive and selective detection of morin with a QY of 15 %. A one-step hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of CQDs from the Acorus calamus rhizome. The fabricated NaBH4-CQD mixture was successfully employed for the catalytic reduction of organic dyes rhodamine B (Rh B) and sunset yellow (SY). The strong fluorescent behaviour of the CQDs was used to explore fluorescent ink applications. The anticancer ability of the CQDs was examined using SKMEL 28 cancer cells. Finally, the progression of apoptosis of SKMEL 28 cells incubated with CQDs was examined by the fluorescence-based staining method.

Acorus calamus rhizome, cefalexin, amoxicillin, vancomycin, kanamycin, 5-fluorouracil, ciprofloxacin, morin, histidine, pyridine, cysteine, quinine sulphate (99.0%), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), rhodamine B (Rh B), sunset yellow (SY), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were obtained from Merck, India. The SKMEL 28 (Human skin cancer cell line) was collected from the National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India.

The CQDs were developed from the natural source Acorus calamus rhizome through a hydrothermal method. Initially, 3 g of Acorus calamus rhizome were added to 100 mL of distilled water and heated to 200 °C for 6 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After being centrifuged at 1500 rpm, the resulting brownish solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The solution was freeze-dried to obtain powdered CQDs, which were then dissolved in distilled water to have 0.30 mg/mL CQD solution. The obtained solution was stored in a refrigerator for further characterization and use. The various stages of the synthesis are depicted in Scheme 1 .

Using quinine sulphate as the standard (QY = 54 %) the quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was determined. The calculations were done based on the equation,
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
μ s
μ R
×
(
η s
η R
)
2
Where ΦS and ΦR stand for the QY of the sample and standard, respectively. The terms μS and μR represent the slopes of integrated emission intensity versus the absorbance plot of the sample and reference, respectively. Refractive indices of the sample and reference are indicated by the symbols ηs and ηR, respectively.

The fluorescence detection experiments were conducted at a pH of 5 and an interaction period of 2 min using 0.30 mg/mL CQD and various doses of morin solutions. The selectivity of the sensor was assessed by introducing a series of interfering molecules in place of morin under identical experimental conditions. To explore the sensitivity of the sensor, anti-interference studies were performed by mixing 25 μM interference compounds into a cuvette containing 5 μM CQD-morin mixture. All the experiments were performed in triplicate at room temperature.

Two major pollutant dyes, rhodamine B (RhB) and sunset yellow (SY) were chosen in the catalytic reduction experiments. During the investigation, 2 mL of 1 mM dye and 0.25 mL of 0.05 M NaBH4 solutions were mixed with 0.5 mL of CQD solutions. UV–vis. absorption spectroscopy was used to assess the catalytic reduction.

The CQD solutions were directly injected into a pen and used as fluorescent ink without any chemical treatment. Under UV light having a wavelength of 360 nm, the text and graphics on the filter paper were clearly visible.

The cellular metabolic activity was measured using MTT assay on SKMEL 28 human skin cancer cells. It is a valuable tool for examining cell viability, proliferation and cytotoxicity. This colorimetric method is based on the reduction of a yellow tetrazolium salt (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide or MTT) to purple formazan crystals by metabolically active cells. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM-Himedia), supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic cocktail containing Penicillin (100 U/mL), Streptomycin (100 μg/mL), and Amphotericin B (2.5 μg/mL). The samples were diluted in DMEM media and added to the wells containing cultured cells with concentrations of 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 μg/mL. Untreated wells were kept as control. All the experiments were done in triplicate, and average values were taken to minimize errors. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured with microplate reader.

The cell viability was determined using the following formula:
Percentage
of
cell
viability =
Average
absorbance
of
treated
Average
absorbance
of
control
× 100

The DNA-binding dyes such as acridine orange (AO) and ethidium bromide (EB) were employed for the morphological detection of apoptotic and necrotic cells. AO is absorbed by both viable and non-viable cells and emits green fluorescence if intercalated into double-stranded nucleic acid (DNA). EB is exclusively taken by non-viable cells and emits red fluorescence by intercalation into DNA. After treatment with various doses of CQDs, the cells were washed by cold PBS and stained with a mixture of AO (100 μg/mL) and EB (100 μg/mL) at room temperature for 10 min. The stained cells were washed twice and observed under a fluorescence microscope in a blue filter.

The optical characteristics of the sensing probe have been examined utilizing absorption and emission spectroscopy methods. The absorbance spectrum (Fig. 1 a) of the CQD reveals two separate absorption maxima centred at 215 and 265 nm [23]. The peak at 215 nm corresponds to the π - π * transition of C=C bonds, while the broader peak at 260 nm could be designated to the n- π * transition of C=O moiety. The CQDs displayed maximum fluorescence emission at 435 nm when exciting the sample at 340 nm (Fig. 1a). The images of CQD solution under visible light (brown) and UV light (blue) is shown as an inset in Fig. 1a. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm is depicted in Fig. 1b. On increasing the excitation wavelength, a red shift in the emission intensity was observed, with the highest intensity at 435 nm under an excitation of 340 nm. The QY of the system was 15 % in comparison with the standard quinine sulphate.

The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) showed that the developed quantum dots are spherical and highly dispersed in water without aggregation. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 2b) exposed the lattice spacing in the CQDs with a fringe width of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the lattice spacing in graphitic carbon atoms [24]. The amorphous behaviour of the CQDs was evident from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern given in Fig. 2c. According to the size distribution histogram in Fig. 2d, the average size of the CQDs was around 6 nm. The average particle size from the dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment was approximately 10.5 nm, comparable to the particle size calculated from the TEM analysis (Fig. S1).

The functional groups on the CQD surface were examined using FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3 a). The characteristic -O-H stretching vibration was observed at 3310 cm−1. A small band at 2965 cm−1 was attributed to the C–H stretching vibration. Bands at 1670 and 1370 cm−1 were ascribed to the C=O and C–O–C bonds, respectively [25]. As evident from Fig. 3b, the XRD pattern of CQDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak centred at 2θ = 25.32°, corresponding to the highly disordered graphitic (002) plane, further confirming the amorphous structure of the developed CQDs [26]. Raman spectrum was recorded to identify the defect in carbon nanomaterial. Raman spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 3c) showed two distinct peaks at 1342 and 1562 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band measures the amount of defects in the graphitic carbon sheets, and the G band originates from the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atom of the graphitic sheet [27]. The intensities of the D and G bands are denoted by ID and IG, respectively. The ID/IG ratio was used to calculate the degree of graphitization of CQDs, which was found to be 0.85. The higher intensity of the G band relative to the D band in the Raman spectrum (ID < IG), confirms the formation of a graphitic structure without any carbonaceous impurities [28].

The elemental composition and surface functional states of the CQDs were examined using the XPS spectral analysis. The survey spectrum reveals that the newly designed sensor comprises the elements C1s (284.6 eV) and O1s (531.5 eV) with a composition of 72 and 28 %, respectively (Fig. 4 a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) displayed three different surface components C–C/C=C (285.2 eV), C–O (285.9 eV) and C=O (288.8 eV). The deconvoluted O1s (Fig. 4c) spectrum produced two prominent oxygen states, including C=O (532.1 eV) and C–O (533.2 eV) [29].

The fluorescence emission behaviour of the CQDs was assessed by altering the ionic strength, pH, visible light irradiation time, and storage time. The stability assessment of CQDs in KCl solutions with varying ionic strengths (0–2 M) is shown in Fig. S2a. Under different ionic strengths, the fluorescence intensity was not significantly changed. The fluorescence stability of the CQDs was tested in the pH range of 3–12 (Fig. S2b), and the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CQD is remarkably stable in acidic, basic, and neutral media. When the sample was continually exposed to visible light for 3 h, the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs did not significantly change (Fig. S2c). In addition, the impact of the storage period on the luminescence properties was investigated by keeping the CQD solution in the refrigerator for three months. The CQDs did not aggregate, and no change in their emission properties was observed (Fig. S2d).

To improve the sensing ability of the developed sensor, experimental conditions such as pH and interaction time were optimized. The change in fluorescence intensity of CQDs when mixed with 5 μM morin at different pH levels (Fig. S3a) revealed that maximum interaction was possible at pH 5. The highest interaction between morin and CQDs occurred within 2 min of mixing as shown in Fig. S3b. Hence, all subsequent analyses were carried out at pH 5 with an interaction time of 2 min.

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded after introducing 5 μM different analytes such as cefalexin, amoxicillin, vancomycin, morin, kanamycin, 5-fluorouracil, histidine, pyridine, cysteine, ciprofloxacin at a pH of 5 within an interaction period of 2 min. As seen in the F/F0 plot in Fig. 5 a only morin molecules effectively suppress the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence and absence of analyte molecules is denoted by the symbols F and F0. The surface of the CQDs is negatively charged as obtained from the zeta potential measurement (Fig. 5b), and the negative surface charge was reduced from −24.0 to −4.7 mV by the interaction of the morin molecules with the surface functional groups on CQDs (Fig. 5c).

The limit of detection (LOD) and linear range of the detection was evaluated using the Stern-Volmer equation,
[1]
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]

The terms F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of morin; the concentrations of morin and Stern-Volmer constant are represented by the symbols [Q] and Ksv, respectively. As evident from Fig. 5d–a linear dependence in the fluorescence intensity was observed on increasing the morin concentration from 0 to 16 μM. The LOD was determined from the F0/F vs concentration of morin plot (Fig. 5e) using the equation,
[2]
LOD = 3 σ / m
Where σ and m denote the standard deviation and slope of the F0/F vs. concentration of the morin plot. The obtained LOD was 96 nM within the linear range of 0–2 μM. Table 1 shows a comparative LOD study of some recently published works with the current work. The cyclic test curves were recorded to analyze the stability and reusability of the designed CQD sensor for the specific recognition of morin molecules. From Fig. S4, the sensing ability of the proposed sensor was unaffected even after four continuous cycles.

The selectivity of the sensor towards morin was examined by adding 25 μM of interfering molecules, including cefalexin, amoxicillin, vancomycin, kanamycin, 5-fluorouracil, histidine, pyridine, cysteine, ciprofloxacin to the CQD-morin mixture having 5 μM morin concentration and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded. As evident from Fig. 6 a, even in the excess concentration of foreign molecules, the proposed system could detect the morin molecules efficiently without interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescent detection of morin
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence lifetime analysis was performed to better understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of morin to CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6b, there was no discernible difference in the fluorescence lifetime before (5.75 ns) and after (5.69 ns) the addition of morin solution to the CQDs. This finding suggests the possibility of a static quenching rather than the dynamic quenching between CQDs and morin. In addition, a new peak at 358 nm appeared in the UV–vis. absorption spectra of the CQD-morin mixture (Fig. S5) compared to that of pure CQDs further confirm the possibility of a static quenching effect [35]. At the same time, a considerable overlap observed between the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CQDs and the absorption spectrum of morin has been identified, suggesting the possibility of an inner filter effect (IFE) between the CQDs and morin (Fig. S5). Hence, the quenching mechanism includes a combination of static quenching and an inner filter effect. CQDs act as energy donors throughout the detection process, whereas morin molecules act as energy acceptors.

A standard addition method was followed for the detection of morin in real samples by mixing human urine spiked with various concentrations of morin and CQDs, and fluorescence analysis was performed. Table 2 summarizes all the experimental results, which show an excellent recovery rate of 98.7–102.2 %. These findings demonstrate the great selectivity, outstanding anti-interference ability, and good repeatability of the developed sensor for detecting morin in complex biological samples.

Recently, CQDs doped with metals like tin, calcium, zinc, etc., have found widespread application in the catalytic reduction of ionic dyes. With the assistance of the reducing agent NaBH4, we have developed a metal-free, cost-effective, and environmental friendly catalyst, namely a CQD-NaBH4 mixture, which has been successfully used for the catalytic reduction of the dyes rhodamine B (Rh B) and sunset yellow (SY) into less harmful products. The optimal concentrations of NaBH4, dyes and CQDS were determined through several experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the absorption peak at 554 nm of Rh B was only slightly diminished on adding NaBH4 alone into the Rh B solution for 30 min. However, the absorption peak intensity decreased rapidly within 180 s with a reduction efficiency of 98.4% when both NaBH4 and CQDs were present in the medium (Fig. 7b). In the case of SY, no significant decrease in the absorption band intensity at 480 nm was observed when NaBH4 alone was added into the reaction medium (Fig. 7c). However, when CQDs and NaBH4 were present together (Fig. 7d), efficient degradation of SY occurred within 120 s, with a reduction efficiency of 99.2 %. According to the -ln (C/C0) vs. time graph (Fig. 7e) the reduction process follows pseudo first-order kinetics with a rate constant of 0.0116 min−1. The reusability of the catalyst was examined by continuously performing the catalytical reduction process for four cycles using the same catalyst (Fig. S6). No considerable decrease in the reduction ability of the catalyst was observed towards the refractory pollutants even after the fourth cycle.

A schematic illustration of the catalytic reduction mechanism of dyes by the combined action of NaBH4 and CQDs is given in Scheme 2 . When NaBH4 was present in the medium alone, the magnitude of the reduction process was completely negligible; however, when NaBH4 and CQDs were added to the dye solutions, the catalytic reduction occurred and was finished within seconds. According to the literature an electron transfer mechanism can be used to explain the catalytic reduction of dyes by the combined action of NaBH4 and CQDs [36,37]. Initially, the BH4 − ions are generated by the dissociation of NaBH4, followed by its adsorption on the CQD surface through an electrostatic interaction between BH4 − ions and functional groups present on the CQD surface. Since the pollutants in this study contain aromatic rings in their structure, they are also easily adsorbed on the CQD surface due to the π-π stacking interaction between the CQDs and aromatic rings of the dye molecules. Then, electrons are transferred from the BH4 − to dyes with the help of CQDs, resulting in the reduction of the dyes. Here, the CQD surface acts as a surface mediator for electron transfer from BH4 − to dyes due to its high surface area and strong electron absorption capacity. Finally, spontaneous desorption of the degradation products into the solution regenerates the catalyst surface for further reactions.

To explore the fluorescence-based applications of CQDs, fluorescent ink was developed by filling a pen with the CQD solution without any chemical treatment and applying it for drawing and writing. As depicted in Fig. 8 , when the figures were exposed to UV light, they were clearly visible with a bright blue fluorescence. The fluorescence stability of the images was examined by keeping the samples for six months and comparing them. Stable and clear fluorescent images were obtained even after six months. This confirms the potential ability of the CQD-based ink as a suitable replacement for conventional fluorescent ink for producing very clear, inexpensive, eco-friendly, distinct and stable fluorescent images [38].

The anticancer potential of Acorus calamus rhizome-derived CQDs was evaluated in SKMEL 28 (human skin melanoma) cells using the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay. Fluorescence microscopic images of SKMEL cells incubated with different concentrations of CQDs (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL-1) shown in Fig. 9 revealed that the anticancer ability of CQDs on SKMEL 28 cells increases in a concentration-dependent manner. In addition, the cytotoxic analysis was also performed on normal fibroblast cells and cancerous SKMEL cells to confirm the specific action of CQDs towards the cancerous cells. The cytotoxicity analysis depicted in Fig. S7, confirms that the cell viability decreases considerably as the CQD concentration increases in the case of cancerous cells, however, the normal living cells are unaffected by the CQDs even at higher concentrations. The relatively low LD50 value of 102 μg/mL against SKMEL 28 cells shows the effectiveness of CQDs against the cancerous cells and the normal cells are unaffected by the CQDs as evident from the relatively high LD50 value of 505 μg/mL.

The apoptosis studies were carried out by following a double staining method using the DNA-binding dyes acridine orange (AO) and ethidium bromide (EB). AO is taken up by both viable and non-viable cells and emits green fluorescence if intercalated into DNA. However, only non-viable cells absorb EB, which intercalates into DNA and emits red fluorescence. After being stained with AO and EB, the cells were observed using a fluorescence microscope with a blue filter. In the apoptosis studies (Fig. 10 ) the living cells show a typical green fluorescence, the early apoptosis cells exhibit bright green fluorescence with condensed or fragmented chromatin, late apoptosis cells display orange-stained nuclei with chromatin condensation or fragmentation, and uniformly orange-stained fluorescence was observed from the late apoptosis cells.

In short, we used a hydrothermal method to fabricate multifunctional CQDs from Acorus calamus rhizomes. The developed CQDs exhibit spherical morphology with an average particle size of 6 nm. The excellent fluorescent behaviour of the CQDs was successfully tuned for the sensitive detection of morin with a LOD of 96 nM. The developed CQDs were successfully applied for the real sample analysis in urine, with the recovery range from 98.7 to 102.2 %. Using the organic dyes rhodamine B (Rh B) and sunset yellow (SY), the catalytic reduction capability of the NaBH4-CQD mixture was assessed. The reaction has pseudo-first-order kinetics with a rate constant of 0.0116 min−1. The fluorescent ink-based applications of CQDs were deeply studied and applied for drawing and writing. The anticancer properties of CQDs were investigated against SKMEL 28 cancer cells. The apoptosis studies for the living and nonliving cells were performed using acridine orange and ethidium bromide with the double staining method. Our work has opened new doors in chemical sensing, catalytic reduction, fluorescent ink, and anticancer applications using CQDs derived from an environmental friendly and green approach.

Bony K. John: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Jincy Mathew: Investigation. Sreekanth K: Data curation, Visualization. Radhakrishnan E. K: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Senior Research Fellowship provided by UGC India (Grant Number BININ01764209).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtsust.2024.100715.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of hydrophilic and hydrophobic fluorescent carbon dots using deep eutectic solvents as designer reaction media
J. Mater. Sci.
